region_of_origin: Global (commercialised in North America and Europe)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for rice milk as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for plant-based milk alternatives is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of rice milk is one glass of approximately 240 ml, equivalent to 240 g, commonly used as a dairy milk substitute in beverages, cereals, or cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Rice milk, fluid — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for rice milk (fluid), cr_id F01020012. All core macros and secondary nutrients sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Rice milk GI of 86 is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care 2008). This is a well-established high-GI value for commercial rice milk. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (86 × 23.69) / 100 ≈ 20.5 for a 240 g serving (23.69 g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (5.29 g/100 g) qualifying as a sugar-sweetened beverage; low sodium (30 mg/100 g) is a partial offset but does not neutralise the SSB risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit rice milk due to its sugar-sweetened beverage status (5.29 g sugar/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold), as dietary sugar independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; unsweetened almond milk or oat milk with higher potassium content are preferred alternatives.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (GI 86) combined with added sugar (5.29 g/100 g) and zero dietary fibre, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Rice milk is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very high GI of 86, added sugar content of 5.29 g/100 g, and complete absence of dietary fibre, all of which drive rapid glucose absorption; patients should substitute with unsweetened soy milk (GI ~34) or unsweetened almond milk (GI ~25) which have substantially lower glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 5.29 g sugar/100 g and negligible saturated fat; low overall fat content is favourable but high sugar intake is an independent CVD risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Rice milk poses a moderate cardiovascular concern primarily through its added sugar content (5.29 g/100 g), which contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial dysfunction at habitual intake levels; its low saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) and absence of cholesterol are favourable, but individuals with CVD should choose unsweetened plant milks to avoid the sugar-driven lipid risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (5.29 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory mediator production; zero omega-3, zero fibre, and negligible antioxidants provide no anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rice milk is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as its added sugar content promotes inflammatory mediator production and it provides no anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, no polyphenols, zero fibre); fortified unsweetened soy milk or almond milk are nutritionally superior alternatives for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rice milk is a dairy-free, plant-based milk alternative produced by blending milled rice with water, then straining to yield a thin, mildly sweet liquid. It is commercially processed and typically contains added sugar, resulting in a sugar content of approximately 5.3 g per 100 g. Per 100 g, rice milk provides 50 kcal, 0.2 g protein, 0.88 g fat, 9.87 g carbohydrate, 5.29 g sugar, 0 g dietary fibre, and 30 mg sodium. Its texture is thinner and less creamy than cow's milk or soy milk, with a light, slightly sweet, neutral flavour that makes it palatable in cereals, hot beverages, and baking. Rice milk is naturally free of dairy, lactose, soy, and nuts, making it suitable for individuals with multiple food allergies, though it is nutritionally sparse compared to other plant milks. Clinically, rice milk carries a very high glycemic index of 86 — among the highest of all plant milks — due to the rapid hydrolysis of rice starch during processing. Combined with its added sugar and complete absence of dietary fibre, it causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation and is contraindicated for Type 2 Diabetes management. Its sugar content of 5.29 g/100 g meets the sugar-sweetened beverage threshold, raising concerns for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Rice milk is vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free, and is widely available in Singapore and globally as a commercial product.
common_user_queries: Is rice milk good for diabetics?
Is rice milk suitable for lactose intolerance?
Rice milk vs soy milk nutrition
meal_context: hot beverage substitute for dairy milk

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F01020012) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 86 is well-documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (International Tables of GI and GL Values, Diabetes Care 2008). Saturated fat estimated at 0.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central rice milk entry (NDB 14639) as HPB does not report fatty acid breakdown; this is documented in notes.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — rice milk contains no dietary fibre by formulation. calcium_mg and iron_mg are confirmed zero per HPB source for this unfortified rice milk variant; consumers should verify packaging as commercial products may be fortified. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based product with no animal-derived ingredients. 2. saturated_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for rice milk (NDB 14639 / FDC ID 174832) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions; this is an adapted value. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific HPB rice milk entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database; USDA FoodData Central provides partial coverage but values vary significantly by brand and fortification status. 3. GI note: the input record incorrectly cited GI 31 (low) from an unverified embedded GI database. The corrected GI of 86 (high) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Foster-Powell et al., Diabetes Care 2008), which is the established peer-reviewed value for commercial rice milk. The health_suitability diabetes entry in the input record correctly identified high GI concern despite the conflicting GI value field. 4. Glycemic load per serving: GL = (86 × 23.69) / 100 = 20.4 ≈ 20.5 for a 240 g serving containing 23.69 g carbohydrate. 5. This record represents a generic commercial rice milk product. Fortified variants may have substantially higher calcium, vitamin D, and vitamin B12 values; consumers should check product labels.
